coredata.dc:title: Defect induced third order optical nonlinearities of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zachariah, Elsa Susan
Mathew, Raji Mary
Jose, Jasmine
John, Josmi
G, Vinitha
Thomas, Vinoy
I, Rejeena
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the effect of defects on the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), synthesized via a hydrothermal method using graphene oxide and ammonia solution. With variation in the ammonia concentration during synthesis, the ID/IG ratio initially decreases, reaches a minimum (1.26 to 0.89) and again increases (0.89 to 1.12). The third order nonlinear susceptibility deduced through Zscan analysis employing continuous wave laser at 532 nm, also follows a similar trend to that of the ID/IG ratio, decreasing to a minimum (1.60 × 10-7 e.s.u to 0.78 × 10-7 e.s.u) before increasing again (0.78 × 10-7 e.s.u to 1.61 × 10-7 e.s.u). The influence of the nonlinear optical properties on the defect level is evident as these parameters exhibit an increasing trend with increase in the degree of defects. The defect-induced nonlinearities of N-GQDs offer potential for photonic applications requiring precise control of nonlinear optical responses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Degree of defects
Zscan
Nonlinear optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)XRD pattern, (b) TEM image with size distribution, (c) HRTEM image and (d) SAED pattern of NGQD3.
(a)UV–Visible absorption spectrum and (b) Photoluminescence spectra of NGQD3 with inset showing the sample under 320 nm excitation.
Raman Spectra of the samples NGQD1 to NGQD5.
Schematic representation of Pyridinic, Pyrrolic ang graphitic nitrogen configurations in the graphitic lattice. Reproduced from (14) with permission from the Royal society of Chemistry.
XPS analysis C1s and N1s spectra of samples NGQD1 to NGQD5((a) to (e)).
Open Aperture Zscan pattern with theoretical fit of samples NGQD1 to NGQD5 ((a) to (e)).
Closed aperture Zscan pattern with theoretical fit of samples NGQD1 to NGQD5 ((a) to (e)).
ID/IG ratio and percentage of graphitic nitrogen in the samples.
Third order nonlinear optical parameters of NGQD samples.

head.ce:title: Defect induced third order optical nonlinearities of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Functional Materials, Department of Physics, Christian College, University of Kerala, Chengannur 689122, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Functional Materials, Department of Physics, Christian College, University of Kerala, Chengannur 689122, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, MSM College, Kayamkulam 690502, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, MSM College, Kayamkulam 690502, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the effect of defects on the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), synthesized via a hydrothermal method using graphene oxide and ammonia solution. With variation in the ammonia concentration during synthesis, the ID/IG ratio initially decreases, reaches a minimum (1.26 to 0.89) and again increases (0.89 to 1.12). The third order nonlinear susceptibility deduced through Zscan analysis employing continuous wave laser at 532 nm, also follows a similar trend to that of the ID/IG ratio, decreasing to a minimum (1.60 × 10-7 e.s.u to 0.78 × 10-7 e.s.u) before increasing again (0.78 × 10-7 e.s.u to 1.61 × 10-7 e.s.u). The influence of the nonlinear optical properties on the defect level is evident as these parameters exhibit an increasing trend with increase in the degree of defects. The defect-induced nonlinearities of N-GQDs offer potential for photonic applications requiring precise control of nonlinear optical responses.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Defect engineering is a well-established strategy for tailoring the properties of nanoscale materials to meet the requirements of specific applications. Defects/ disorder present in nanoscale systems alter their local electronic structure giving rise to significant effects on their electronic, magnetic and optical properties[1]. Nanoforms of graphene-based materials have proven to possess multifaceted properties and application potential in view of their strong light −matter interaction. Nonlinear optical properties of graphene derivatives such as graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has garnered interest in researchers worldwide in view of their extended π-conjugate system. Any alteration or defects introduced into this system can significantly affect their nonlinear responses. The effect of the nonlinear optical properties on defects in graphene-based structures such as GO and reduced rGO has been extensively studied. These defects which are introduced as sp3 carbon atoms into the sp2 carbon lattice can be tuned with synthesis conditions such as synthesis time, temperature etc. Tuneable saturable absorption with layer stacking in polycrystalline graphene was demonstrated which can be used for various photonic applications [2]. Nonlinear absorption coefficient of GO and rGO with varying degrees of sp2/sp3 carbon configurations produced by solvothermal treatment at different reduction temperatures reveal that sp2 carbon configurations dominate the nonlinear reverse saturable absorption response[3]. Third order nonlinear optical properties of defect enriched GO and rGO were studied using Zscan technique employing continuous wave laser at 532 nm. The study revealed an increase in nonlinear behaviour with defects/disorder[4]. There have been a few studies on the effect of defects on the nonlinear response in GO and rGO, however the effect of defect levels on the nonlinear properties of graphene quantum dots (GQD) have not yet been reported.

Various techniques can be employed for the elucidation of the nonlinear optical parameters such as nonlinear interferometry, degenerate four wave mixing, almost degenerate three wave mixing, Zscan method etc. Out of these, Zscan analysis technique developed by Sheikh-Bahe et.al is the most simple and efficient tool for measuring third order nonlinear optical parameters of a material [5]. Zscan technique measures the phase shift generated in a laser beam as it propagates through a nonlinear medium from which nonlinear refractive index can be calculated. Moreover, Zscan can also measure change in laser transmission caused by nonlinear absorption of the material. Thus Zscan remains to be a simple technique which can rapidly determine the nonlinear absorption (NLA) and nonlinear refraction (NLR) in nonlinear materials[6]. The current work thus employs Zscan analysis to elucidate the optical nonlinearity of N-GQD and explore the effect of defects on their nonlinear optical parameters. A clear understanding of the influence of defects on the optical nonlinearity opens up scope for defect engineering of zero-dimensional graphene structures to widen their photonic application.

Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) were synthesised using hydrothermal reduction of GO in the presence of ammonia solution. The analytical grade chemicals used were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and Nice Chemicals.

Modified hummers method was used for the successful synthesis of GO [7], which was further hydrothermally treated in the presence of ammonia solution to produce NGQD as previously reported [8]. Varying degrees of defects in NGQD were achieved through the variation of ammonia solution. In short aqueous solution of GO along with varying amounts of ammonia solution (100 μl,250 μl, 500 μl, 750 μl and 1 ml) were treated in a Teflon lined autoclave at 180οC for 6 h. The resulting mixtures after natural cooling were filtered and centrifuged to remove the larger particles. The solution was neutralized by heating at 90οC to obtain NGQD solutions. The samples were named respectively as NGQD1, NGQD2, NGQD3, NGQD4 and NGQD5.

Xray diffraction (XRD) studies were carried out using PANALYTICAL Xray Diffractometer using Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) radiation. JEOL 2100 Transmission Electron Microscope was used for procuring transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and Witec Aplha300 RA-Confocal Raman Microscope for raman analysis of the samples. Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 was utilized for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The optical properties were analysed using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy with a UV 1800 Spectrophotometer and photoluminescence spectroscopy with a JASCO-FP 8500 Spectrophotometer.

Nonlinear optical properties of NGQD samples were recorded using Zscan analysis. For the analysis, a Diode Pumped Solid State (DPSS) laser operating at 532 nm with a power output of 100 mW was utilized.

The XRD pattern of the NGQD3 (Fig. 1 (a)) shows a peak at 27.2 degree corresponding to the (002) graphitic plane with a d-spacing of 0.33 nm showing the successful reduction of GO into GQD structures [9]. The XRD pattern of GO and samples NGQD1, NGQD2, NGQD4 and NGQD5 are given in Fig. S1 and S2 respectively. The TEM image shown in Fig. 1 (b) reveals NGQD with an average size distribution of 2.95 nm. The High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image shown in Fig. 1 (c) and Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 1 (d) indicate a d-spacing value of 0.33 nm in accordance with XRD pattern. Thus, the structural analysis confirms the successful exfoliation and reduction of GO into GQD.

UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy were used to probe the linear optical properties of NGQD. The UV–Visible absorption spectra of all samples presents two prominent bands centered at 230 nm and 331 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of the graphitic core and n-π* transitions of C-N and C-O bonds [10]. Fig. 2 (a) presents the UV–Visible absorption spectra of NGQD3 and the spectra of all other samples are included in Fig. S3(a). All the samples exhibit excitation independent photoluminescent emission at 428 nm at an excitation of 320 nm consistent with blue-emission of GQD reported [11].The photoluminescence spectra of NGQD3 is shown in Fig. 2 (b) and that of NGQD1, NGQD2, NGQD4 and NGQD5 are include in Fig. S3 (b). The photoluminescence excitation spectra present two major peaks at 270 nm and 330 nm which corresponds to UV–visible absorption bands at 230 nm and 331 nm respectively. The quantum yield of NGQD3 measured using quinine sulphate as reference using standard procedures, was obtained as 7.05 % which aligns with reported values [12].

Raman spectroscopy is a potent analysis method for the determination of oxidation degree and revealing the structure in graphene-based structures. The raman spectra of the carbon structures consists of two characteristic peaks, the G band centered around 1591 cm−1 corresponding to the ordered, crystalline and less defective structures and the D-band centered around 1361 cm−1 corresponding to the discorded defective structures [13]. Thus, G band attributes the sp2 structure while the D band attribute the defects structure relating to sp3 carbon structures. The degree of disorder in graphene-based structures can be elucidated from ID/IG ratio where ID and IG represent integrated intensities of D and G bands respectively[14]. Fig. 3 shows the Raman Spectra of the samples NGQD1 to NGQD5 with variations in the ID/IG ratio. Further additional bands around 1100cm-1 for the samples NGQD1, NGQD3 and NGQD5 seems to be originating from the background glass substrate [15]. The ID/IG ratio of NGQD1 is 1.26 displaying a higher order of disorder. The ratio slowly decreases till NGQD3 and then again increases with NGQD5 displaying a ratio of 1.12. This trend in the ID/IG ratio is attributed to the change in the mechanism of nitrogen introduction into the graphene structure with change in the ammonia concentration. This is confirmed with XPS analysis and is explained below.

Generally, three different mechanism of nitrogen introduction into carbon structures are reported which are pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen. The pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen is bonded to two and one carbon atom respectively and lies predominantly at the edges while graphitic nitrogen replaces a carbon in the graphene lattice. Thus, pyridinic and graphitic nitrogen are sp2 hybridised while pyrrolic nitrogen is sp3 hybridised[16,17].The three main configurations of nitrogen doping are depicted in Fig. 4 .

The XPS analysis reveals elemental contribution from carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Fig. 5 shows the deconvoluted high resolution C1s and N1s peaks of NGQD1 to NGQD5 ((a) to (e)). The C1s spectra is deconvoluted into three components which are C=C,C-OH and C-N bonds centered at ∼ 284 eV, 285 eV and 287 eV respectively [18]. Similarly, the N1s spectra is resolved into three components which are pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen,cantered ∼ 396 eV, 397 eV and 399 eV respectively. These peaks are attributed to the different mechanisms in which nitrogen is doped into graphitic lattice. As evidenced from the XPS analysis, most of the nitrogen is introduced as either pyrrolic or graphitic nitrogen in the samples. Initially, at very low concentrations of ammonia (NGQD1), the pyrrolic nitrogen dominates. With increasing ammonia concentration, the graphitic nitrogen increases and pyrrolic nitrogen concentration decreases. The graphitic nitrogen contribution peaks for NGQD3. With further increase in ammonia concentration, the graphitic nitrogen contribution decreases while the pyrrolic nitrogen contribution increases. At lower concentration of ammonia, nitrogen is introduced into the graphene lattice without changing the sp2 structure, but high concentration of ammonia lead to nitrogen induced defects of the graphene lattice [19]. This correlates with ID/IG variation of the samples from Raman analysis. Table 1 shows the variation of ID/IG ratio and graphitic nitrogen contribution from Raman and XPS analysis respectively.

Zscan analysis is a technique for elucidating the nonlinear optical interactions of a medium through a simple and efficient route. The basic principle of this technique involves translating the medium across a focused laser beam and recording the changes in transmittance of the laser with respect to the position of the sample. This will give information regarding the intensity dependent nonlinear interactions of the sample such as nonlinear absorption and nonlinear refraction[20].

The open aperture (OA) Zscan mode in which laser transmittance through the sample with respect to position of the sample is measured in the absence of aperture, gives information regarding nonlinear absorption of the material. Materials exhibiting saturable absorption (SA) have ground state absorption cross-section greater than the excited state absorption cross section while materials exhibiting reverse saturable absorption (RSA) have higher excited state absorption cross section than ground state absorption cross-section. Thus, SA and RSA materials are characterized by a laser transmittance maximum and minimum at the focus respectively in OA mode.

Nonlinear absorption coefficient β is obtained from the theoretical fitting of the OA data with the relation,
T
( z )
=
∑
m = 0
∞
-
q 0
( z , 0 )
m
m + 1
3 / 2
Where
T ( z )
is the normalized transmittance and
q 0
= β
I 0
L
eff
/
1 +
z
2
/
z
0
2
in which the effective length of the sample given by
L
eff
=
1 -
e
- α l
/ α
, l is the thickness of the sample cell and α is the linear absorption coefficient of the material.
I 0
is the laser intensity at the focus and
z 0
= π
ω
0
2
/ λ
is the diffraction length of the beam with
ω 0
being the laser beam radius at the focal point and λ the wavelength of the laser. m stands for the order of the multiphoton absorption process,
m =
1 , 2
and so on [21]. The nonlinear absorption coefficients of the samples were obtained by fitting equation (1) with m = 1 (two-photon absorption) which was found to be the best fit. The OA Zscan data of the samples revealing the reverse saturable absorption of the material and their corresponding theoretical fit is shown in in Fig. 6 .

The closed aperture (CA) mode of Zscan in which the laser transmittance through an aperture is measured in the far field with respect to position is suitable for determining the nonlinear refractive index of the material. CA scan of a material with self-focusing nonlinearity or positive nonlinear refractive index (n2 > 0) results in a valley followed by peak in the normalized transmittance. When such a sample is placed before the focus, it causes the shift of laser focus nearer to the sample causing more far field divergence of laser light causing reduced aperture transmittance. While as the sample is moved after the focus, the positive lensing effect causes less far field divergence of laser light and hence higher aperture transmittance. This results in the valley followed by peak pattern in normalized transmittance for CA Zscan for a material with positive nonlinear refractive index. For a material exhibiting self-defocusing or negative nonlinear refractive index the opposite happens resulting in a peak followed by valley CA Zscan pattern of normalized transmittance[22]. When continuous wave (CW) or nanosecond pulsed lasers are used for the analysis, absorption of light causes local heating in the absorbing medium. Radial diffusion of heat leads to a spatially varying temperature field which is not proportional to the local light intensity. Thus, the nonlocal dependence of refractive index on the light intensity causes nonlinearity. Taking into consideration the thermal effects arising from CW lasers used in the present study, nonlinear refractive index is evaluated by theoretical fitting using the thermal lens model proposed by Cuppo et.al [23]. Moreover, the CA Zscan data is contributed by both nonlinear refraction and nonlinear absorption; hence the effect of nonlinear refraction can be elucidated from the ratio of normalized transmittance in CA mode to that in OA mode. The nonlinear refractive index of the samples is evaluated by theoretical fitting of the CA/OA Zscan data with the thermal lens equation.

Which is given by,

Δ ϕ ∗
2 x

Where

is on-axis phase shift and
x = z /
z 0
.

Δ ϕ
is related to the nonlinear refractive index, n2 as
Δ ϕ = k
L
eff
n 2
I 0
The CA/OA Zscan with the corresponding theoretical fitting is shown in Fig. 7 . The self focusing nature of the materials with negative nonlinear refractive index is evident from CA/OA curves.

Third order nonlinear optical susceptibility can be calculated from their real and imaginary parts which are given by the relations,
Re
(
χ
3
)
( e s u )
=
( 10
- 4
ε 0
c
2
n
0
2
/ π )
n 2
cm
2
/ W
and
Im
(
χ
3
)
( e s u )
=
( 10
- 2
ε 0
n
0
2
λ /
4 π
2
) β
c m / W
The third order nonlinear optical susceptibility
χ
3
can be thus calculated as
χ
3
=
Re (
χ
3
)
2
+
Im (
χ
3
)
2
Table 2 displays the nonlinear optical parameters of NGQD1 to NGQD5.

The effect of defects on the nonlinear optical response of graphene-based structures is evident from the study. β ,
n 2
and
χ
3
initially decreases for the samples NGQD1 to NGQD3 and thereafter increases for the samples NGQD4 to NGQD5 in accordance with defect structures characterized by Raman spectroscopy and XPS analysis. The effect of defects on the nonlinear optical response can be understood on the basis of the amorphous structural model of graphene-based structures. According to this model, GO is comprised of small sp2 carbon configurations and sp2 carbon domains in a sp3 carbon matrix. These different regions correspond to different electronic structures such as; sp3 matrix typically possess a σ-σ* bandgap of ∼ 6 eV, sp2 clusters normally have a semiconductor bandgap of ∼ 0.5 eV while sp2 domains energy varies from 0.5 to 6 eV depending on size [24].During the oxidation process of GO formation from graphite, defects in the form of oxygen containing functional groups are introduced into the graphene lattice thereby producing sp2 carbon clusters which are well-formed bunches of aromatic rings in the sp3 matrix[25]. On hydrothermal reduction of GO in the presence of lower concentrations of ammonia solution, smaller sp2 domains form and grow within the sp3 matrix, leading to decrease in ID/IG value. Also, nitrogen doping in these concentrations are introduced as graphitic nitrogen which contributes to the decrease in ID/IG ratio. However, for higher concentrations of ammonia solution, nitrogen is introduced into the structure as pyrrolic nitrogen contributing to the defects and reduction in sp2 domains. Sine the excitation energy used in the current Zscan setup, is 2.33 eV (532 nm) which is below the sp3 matrix band gap and well above the sp2 cluster bandgap, it follows that two −photon absorption (TPA) mainly arises from the sp3 matrix. This explains the variation of nonlinear absorption coefficient with ID/IG ratio which is a direct measure of the sp3 carbon configuration.

The nonlinear refractive index
n 2
, also shows a similar trend with ID/IG ratio. The mechanism of nonlinear refraction utilizing continuous wave lasers is thermal in origin [2627]. The continuous propagation of laser through the medium results in a dominant nonlocal interaction leading to generation of heat in the medium. Linear or nonlinear absorption followed by nonradiative relaxations to the ground state leads to a thermal induced nonlinearity. The heating causes a heat diffusion leading to a spatial temperature distribution within the sample. Thus leading to positive or negative lensing effect depending on the nonlinear refractive index of the sample[27]. The effect of ID/IG ratio or defects in the medium to the nonlinear refractive index may be attributed to the fact that more defects cause more nonradiative relaxation which in turn leads to enhanced nonlinear refraction. Thus, it is evident that the third order nonlinear optical properties of GQD depends on the degree of the defects in the system and hence can be used for the fine tuning of nonlinear optical parameters for photonic applications.

Defect dependent third order nonlinear optical properties of NGQD were successfully studied using Zscan analysis employing a continuous wave laser operating at 532 nm. The degree of defects was successfully quantified through the ID/IG ratio from Raman spectroscopy and percentage of graphitic nitrogen contribution from XPS analysis. The defect levels initially decrease with increase in concentration of ammonia solution, reaches a minimum and increases with further increase in ammonia concentrations. The nonlinear optical parameters such as β ,
n 2
and
χ
3
shows a direct relationship with defect levels with the values decreasing, reaching a minimum and then increasing with ammonia concentration. These findings highlight the influence of the defect structure on the nonlinear optical responses of graphene quantum dots. Thus, synthesis parameters can be fine adjusted to achieve customizable nonlinear optical parameters for photonic application.

Elsa Susan Zachariah: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Raji Mary Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jasmine Jose: Writing – review & editing. Josmi John: Writing – review & editing. Vinitha G: Writing – review & editing. Vinoy Thomas: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Rejeena I: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
